runs.reasoningSummary: My prior is that top-level Armington elasticities for U.S. aggregate trade are usually calibrated in the low single digits, with substantial uncertainty across datasets and model specifications. I center the distribution around 2.5, allow moderate mass around 2 to 4, and keep a long upper tail for calibrations that use much higher substitutability.
runs.rawResponse: {"interpretation":"Top-level import-versus-domestic Armington elasticity for U.S. aggregate trade or CGE calibration.","point_estimate":2.5,"quantiles":{"p05":1,"p25":1.8,"p50":2.5,"p75":4,"p95":8},"citations":["Armington (1969)","Bergstrand, Egger & Larch (2013)","Feenstra, Obstfeld & Russ (2015)"],"reasoning_summary":"My prior is that top-level Armington elasticities for U.S. aggregate trade are usually calibrated in the low single digits, with substantial uncertainty across datasets and model specifications. I center the distribution around 2.5, allow moderate mass around 2 to 4, and keep a long upper tail for calibrations that use much higher substitutability."}

runs.interpretation: Top-level Armington elasticity between aggregate imports and domestically produced goods for U.S. aggregate trade or CGE calibration.
citations: McDaniel and Balistreri U.S. CGE calibration discussions
Feenstra, Obstfeld, and others on trade elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: I think the top-nest Armington elasticity for U.S. aggregate trade is usually calibrated in the low-single-digit range, with substantial uncertainty because estimates vary by sector, time period, and whether the model uses short-run or long-run substitution. I center it around 2.5, with a fairly wide upper tail to allow for common CGE calibrations near 4 and occasional higher values.

I center it around 2.5, with a fairly wide upper tail to allow for common CGE calibrations near 4 and occasional higher values."}

runs.reasoningSummary: My prior is that the top-level Armington elasticity in U.S.-style aggregate CGE work is typically in the low single digits, with substantial uncertainty because calibrations vary by model, sector aggregation, and whether the parameter is estimated or imposed. I center it around 2.5, with a fairly wide upper tail to reflect some models using much larger values.
